title: Lechón asado
subtitle: Cuban slow-roasted pork shoulder in citrus and garlic mojo
excerpt: Pork shoulder marinated overnight in Cuban mojo (garlic, orange juice, lime, cumin, and oregano), then slow-roasted until the outside is crisp and the inside pulls apart. The centrepiece of Cuban cooking.
sourceNotes: Lechón asado is the Cuban slow-roasted pork, traditionally a whole suckling pig (lechón) cooked in a pit or on a spit at large gatherings, particularly at Christmas. The home version uses a pork shoulder marinated in mojo criollo, a Cuban citrus and garlic marinade. The mojo combines sour orange (naranja agria), garlic, cumin, oregano, and olive oil; sour orange is rarely available in the UK so a combination of fresh orange juice and lime juice is the standard substitute. The pork should be very crispy on the outside and pull-apart tender within.

content.text: Lechón asado is pork cooked the Cuban way. It marinates for a day in mojo criollo, a marinade built on garlic, sour orange, and cumin. Then it roasts slowly until the meat is pull-apart tender and the outside is deeply caramelised and crisp. The mojo is the key. It is both marinade and sauce. Pork without it is just roast pork.

Sour orange (naranja agria) is the traditional citrus and is rarely available outside specialist shops; a combination of orange juice and lime is a workable substitute that captures the same balance of sweet and sharp. The pork needs at least 12 hours in the marinade. The slow roast at 160°C takes 4 to 4.5 hours; the final blast at 220°C crisps the surface.

Blend the {{garlic}} of garlic with the {{orange-juice}} of orange juice, the juice of the {{lime}} limes, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{oregano-dried}} of oregano, {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt to a smooth sauce. Score the {{pork-shoulder}} of pork shoulder all over with a knife, cutting 2 to 3 cm deep. Rub the mojo into the scores and all over the surface. Cover and refrigerate for at least 12 hours.

Remove the pork from the fridge 1 hour before cooking. Preheat the oven to 160°C fan. Place the pork in a roasting tin skin-side up and pour any remaining marinade around it. Cover tightly with foil and roast for 4 to 4.5 hours until the meat pulls apart easily with two forks. Remove the foil, increase the oven to 220°C fan, and roast for a further 20 to 25 minutes until the skin is deeply golden and crisp. Rest for 20 minutes before pulling the meat.

items.fix: Pat the skin dry with kitchen paper before the final blast at high heat. The skin needs to be dry to crisp. If the marinade has pooled under the pork, baste it away from the skin.
items.cause: Skin still wet from the marinade; foil not removed soon enough
items.symptom: Skin not crisping up at high heat

items.fix: The foil must seal the roasting tin completely. If pork shoulder at 160°C emerges dry, it was not sealed properly and lost steam.
items.cause: Foil seal not tight; cooking at too high a temperature
items.symptom: Meat dry despite long cooking time

items.fix: Score the meat to 3 cm depth so the mojo reaches the interior. 12 hours is the minimum; 24 hours is noticeably better.
items.cause: Marinade time too short; not scored deeply enough
items.symptom: Mojo flavour faint in the finished pork

items.fix: Use equal parts orange and lime juice. The orange juice should be the dominant citrus. It tempers the sharpness of the lime.
items.cause: Too much lime juice in the mojo

content.text: Lechón asado is the centrepiece of the Cuban Christmas table. The whole pig is roasted overnight in a pit called a caja china, and eaten with moros y cristianos, yuca con mojo, and fried plantain. In the home kitchen version the shoulder replaces the whole pig and the oven replaces the pit, but the mojo and the principle stay the same. The citrus-garlic pairing is the signature of Cuban cooking and runs through dozens of dishes. Here it is at its strongest, since the pork has soaked it up over a day before the heat changes it.
